mnemonics.title: PRODUCER CONSUMER SEMAPHORE RULES
mnemonics.content: WAIT BEFORE PRODUCING (empty must be > 0), SIGNAL AFTER PRODUCING (increase full), WAIT BEFORE CONSUMING (full must be > 0), SIGNAL AFTER CONSUMING (increase empty), MUTEX PROTECTS BUFFER ACCESS ALWAYS

mnemonics.title: DINING PHILOSOPHERS DEADLOCK PREVENTION
mnemonics.content: BREAK CIRCULAR WAIT: ODD PHILOSOPHERS LEFT THEN RIGHT, EVEN PHILOSOPHERS RIGHT THEN LEFT - OR USE RESOURCE HIERARCHY (always pick lower numbered fork first)

mnemonics.title: READERS WRITERS SOLUTION CHECKLIST
mnemonics.content: READCOUNT TRACKS READERS, MUTEX PROTECTS READCOUNT, RW_MUTEX FOR DATA ACCESS, READERS PREFER: CHECK IF WRITER WAITING BEFORE JOINING, WRITERS PREFER: NO NEW READERS WHEN WRITER WAITING

acronyms.usage: In bounded buffer of size N, max N items can be stored
Points to next position where producer will add an item
Points to next position from which consumer will remove item
Tracks how many readers are currently accessing the shared resource
Binary semaphore controlling access to the shared data for both readers and writers

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a circle with N positions. Producer moves clockwise inserting items at IN pointer. Consumer moves clockwise removing items at OUT pointer. Both pointers wrap around after reaching end.
Draw a round table with 5 philosophers alternating with 5 forks. Each philosopher can only reach forks on immediate left and right. Draw arrows showing dependency cycle.
Draw a shop with one barber chair and N waiting chairs. Customer flow: arrives -> checks waiting chairs -> if empty sits in waiting chair OR leaves -> barber checks for customer -> serves -> repeats.
Draw a database (shared resource) with a gate. Readers form a line that can merge (enter together). Writers must wait until all readers exit, then get exclusive access (gate closed to readers).

analogies.analogy: Think of a restaurant kitchen - chefs (producers) place prepared dishes on a counter (buffer), and waiters (consumers) pick up dishes to serve. Counter has limited space (bounded buffer).
Think of two people sharing one chopstick - each needs both to eat. If both pick up their right chopstick simultaneously, neither can eat. Solution: have one person pick left first.
A library database - many people can read (browse) the books simultaneously, but only one person can write (update) at a time to prevent conflicts.
A car service center - mechanic (barber) sleeps when no cars (customers) arrive. When cars arrive, mechanic wakes up and services one at a time. Other cars wait in queue.
A parking lot with limited spaces - the counter shows available spots. Each car entering (wait) decreases count, each car leaving (signal) increases count. When full, new cars must wait.
